rescue_reasoning: Keep because the text is explicitly about resource efficiency: policy_statement: 'Reduce waste and water use.' verbatim_text: 'REDUCE WASTE AND WATER USE.' This directly supports the Resource Efficiency category and the mechanism waste_and_water_use_reduction → emissions_and_resource_use_reduction. The gate failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token found') appears to be a tokenization/keyword mismatch rather than lack of climate/resource-efficiency relevance. Climate screen is 'explicit_self', which confirms inclusion.
policy_statement: Approach 7: Reduce waste and water use.
mechanism_description: Reduce waste generation and water use to lower associated emissions and overall resource consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service/output → ↓ material & water throughput → ↓ energy use & process emissions (incl. waste handling) → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Households, businesses, and municipal operations generate less solid waste and consume less potable/non-potable water through conservation and waste-prevention practices. (2) Mediating physical/process mechanisms: Less demand for raw material extraction/manufacturing and less pumping/treatment/distribution of water reduces electricity and fuel use; reduced wastewater volumes lower treatment energy; reduced waste volumes lower collection/transport energy and reduce landfill/incineration-related emissions (including methane where organics are avoided). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions and reduced overall resource consumption intensity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from producing, treating, transporting, and disposing of fewer materials and less water.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: delivering the same level of services (sanitation, consumption, water-using activities) with less material and water input, which reduces energy use and associated GHG emissions across supply chains.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the endpoint includes “↓ emissions” (mitigation), the defining mechanism is efficiency/throughput reduction (using fewer resources for the same service), which fits Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance focused mainly on capturing landfill methane or switching to renewable power at water plants, that would shift the dominant pathway toward Mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly about reducing waste/water use (demand/throughput), keeping Resource Efficiency dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that reduces waste generation and/or water use inherently reduces material/water throughput and typically reduces associated energy/process emissions, so Resource Efficiency consistently captures the primary mechanism.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., whether benefits are mainly from avoided landfill methane vs. avoided electricity at water/wastewater facilities) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may be framed or designed primarily as direct GHG abatement (e.g., landfill diversion targeting methane, or specific treatment-plant decarbonization). Those would warrant Mitigation at the policy level, but the stated mechanism (waste and water use reduction) is most generally Resource Efficiency.
co_benefits: Reduced utility costs; improved water security; reduced landfill burden and local pollution
instance_edge_case_notes: High-level approach title with no specified instrument (e.g., pricing, mandate, standard), so classification defaults to a planning/strategy signal rather than a concrete policy tool.
